docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to m9m! This guide will help you get up and running quickly.

docs.title: What is m9m?
docs.text:
m9m is a high-performance workflow automation platform that provides:

  • n8n Compatibility - Run existing n8n workflows without modification
  • Superior Performance - 5-10x faster execution than n8n
  • Lower Resource Usage - 70% less memory, 75% smaller containers
  • Cloud Native - Built for modern infrastructure

docs.title: Option 1: Binary Installation
docs.text:
# Install with Go
go install github.com/neul-labs/m9m/cmd/m9m@latest

# Start the server
m9m serve

docs.title: Option 2: Docker
docs.text:
# Run with Docker
docker run -p 8080:8080 ghcr.io/neul-labs/m9m:latest

docs.title: Option 3: From Source
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/neul-labs/m9m.git
cd m9m

# Build
make build

# Run
./m9m serve

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Once running, verify m9m is working:

# Check health endpoint
curl http://localhost:8080/health

# Expected response:
# {"status":"ok"}

docs.title: Access Points
docs.text:
After starting m9m, you can access:

Service URL Description Web UI http://localhost:8080 Visual workflow editor REST API http://localhost:8080/api/v1 Programmatic access Health http://localhost:8080/health Health check endpoint Metrics http://localhost:8080/metrics Prometheus metrics

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Installation - Detailed installation options
  2. First Workflow - Create your first workflow
  3. Core Concepts - Understand workflows, nodes, and data flow

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: This guide explains the fundamental concepts in m9m workflow automation.

docs.title: Workflows
docs.text:
A workflow is a directed graph of nodes that defines an automated process. Workflows consist of:

  • Nodes - Individual processing steps
  • Connections - Data flow paths between nodes
  • Settings - Workflow-level configuration

docs.title: Workflow Structure
docs.text:
{
  "name": "Example Workflow",
  "nodes": [...],
  "connections": {...},
  "settings": {...},
  "active": true
}

docs.title: Workflow States
docs.text: State Description active Workflow is enabled and can be triggered inactive Workflow is disabled

docs.title: Nodes
docs.text: Nodes are the building blocks of workflows. Each node performs a specific operation.

docs.title: Node Structure
docs.text:
{
  "id": "unique-id",
  "name": "Display Name",
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
  "position": [x, y],
  "parameters": {...},
  "disabled": false
}

docs.title: Node Categories
docs.text: Category Description Examples Core Workflow control Start Trigger Start workflows Webhook, Cron Transform Data manipulation Set, Filter, Code HTTP Web requests HTTP Request Database Data storage PostgreSQL, MySQL Messaging Communication Slack, Discord AI AI/ML operations OpenAI, Anthropic Cloud Cloud services AWS S3, Azure Blob VCS Version control GitHub, GitLab

docs.title: Node Parameters
docs.text:
Parameters configure node behavior. They support:

  • Static values: "url": "https://api.example.com"
  • Expressions: "url": "={{ $json.endpoint }}"

docs.title: Data Items
docs.text: Data flows through workflows as items. Each item is a JSON object.

docs.title: Item Structure
docs.text:
{
  "json": {
    "field1": "value1",
    "field2": 123
  },
  "binary": {
    "data": "base64-encoded..."
  }
}

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐
│  Node A │────▶│  Node B │────▶│  Node C │
│         │     │         │     │         │
│ Output: │     │ Input:  │     │ Input:  │
│ [item1] │     │ [item1] │     │ [item2] │
│ [item2] │     │ [item2] │     │         │
└─────────┘     │ Output: │     └─────────┘
                │ [item2] │
                └─────────┘
  • Nodes receive all items from connected upstream nodes
  • Nodes can output zero, one, or many items
  • Data is passed by reference (not copied)

docs.title: Connections
docs.text: Connections define how data flows between nodes.

docs.title: Connection Structure
docs.text:
{
  "Source Node Name": {
    "main": [
      [
        {"node": "Target Node", "type": "main", "index": 0}
      ]
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Connection Types
docs.text: Type Description main Primary data flow

docs.title: Multiple Outputs
docs.text:
Some nodes have multiple outputs (e.g., Switch node):

{
  "Switch": {
    "main": [
      [{"node": "Path A", "type": "main", "index": 0}],
      [{"node": "Path B", "type": "main", "index": 0}]
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Expressions
docs.text: Expressions allow dynamic values based on input data.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
// Full expression
={{ $json.fieldName }}

// Template string
Hello {{ $json.name }}!

// With operations
={{ $json.price * 1.1 }}

docs.title: Available Variables
docs.text:
Variable Description $json Current item's JSON data $item Current item index $now Current timestamp $env Environment variables $input All input items

See Expressions for complete documentation.

docs.title: Executions
docs.text: An execution is a single run of a workflow.

docs.title: Execution States
docs.text: State Description pending Queued, waiting to run running Currently executing success Completed successfully failed Completed with errors cancelled Manually cancelled

docs.title: Execution Data
docs.text:
Each execution stores:

  • Input data
  • Output data from each node
  • Error messages (if failed)
  • Timing information
  • Execution mode (manual, scheduled, webhook)

docs.title: Credentials
docs.text: Credentials securely store authentication data for external services.

docs.title: Credential Structure
docs.text:
{
  "name": "My API Key",
  "type": "apiKey",
  "data": {
    "apiKey": "secret-value"
  }
}

docs.title: Credential Types
docs.text:
Type Used For apiKey API key authentication oauth2 OAuth 2.0 flows basicAuth HTTP Basic Auth httpHeader Custom headers

Credentials are encrypted at rest and injected into node parameters at runtime.

docs.title: Triggers
docs.text: Triggers start workflow execution.

docs.title: Trigger Types
docs.text: Trigger Description Manual Started via API or CLI Webhook HTTP request to endpoint Schedule Cron-based timing

docs.title: Webhook Triggers
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.webhook",
  "parameters": {
    "path": "/my-webhook",
    "httpMethod": "POST"
  }
}

Endpoint: http://localhost:8080/webhook/my-webhook

docs.title: Scheduled Triggers
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.cron",
  "parameters": {
    "cronExpression": "0 9 * * MON"
  }
}

docs.title: Job Queue
docs.text: m9m uses a job queue for workflow execution.

docs.title: Queue Behavior
docs.text:
1. Workflow execution request → Job created
  2. Job queued with status pending
  3. Worker picks up job → status running
  4. Execution completes → status success or failed

docs.title: Queue Types
docs.text: Type Persistence Use Case memory No Development, testing sqlite Yes Production (single node)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Concept Description Workflow Directed graph of automated steps Node Single processing operation Connection Data flow path between nodes Item Unit of data (JSON object) Expression Dynamic value using input data Execution Single workflow run Credential Secure authentication storage Trigger Workflow start mechanism Queue Execution job management

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Create workflows
  • Explore nodes
  • Learn expressions

docs.title: First Workflow
docs.text: This guide walks you through creating and running your first m9m workflow.

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text:
A simple workflow that:

  1. Starts with a trigger
  2. Makes an HTTP request to fetch data
  3. Transforms the response
  4. Outputs the result

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• m9m installed and running (m9m serve)
  • curl for API calls (or use the Web UI)

docs.title: Step 1: Create the Workflow
docs.text:
Create a file named my-first-workflow.json:

{
  "name": "My First Workflow",
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "start",
      "name": "Start",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.start",
      "position": [250, 300],
      "parameters": {}
    },
    {
      "id": "http",
      "name": "Fetch Posts",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
      "position": [450, 300],
      "parameters": {
        "url": "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1",
        "method": "GET"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "set",
      "name": "Format Output",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.set",
      "position": [650, 300],
      "parameters": {
        "assignments": [
          {
            "name": "title",
            "value": "={{ $json.title }}"
          },
          {
            "name": "summary",
            "value": "Post ID: {{ $json.id }} by User {{ $json.userId }}"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ],
  "connections": {
    "Start": {
      "main": [[{"node": "Fetch Posts", "type": "main", "index": 0}]]
    },
    "Fetch Posts": {
      "main": [[{"node": "Format Output", "type": "main", "index": 0}]]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Using the CLI
docs.text:
m9m run my-first-workflow.json

Expected output:

{
  "status": "success",
  "data": [
    {
      "title": "sunt aut facere repellat provident occaecati excepturi optio reprehenderit",
      "summary": "Post ID: 1 by User 1"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Using the API
docs.text:
First, create the workflow:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/workflows \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @my-first-workflow.json

Then execute it:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/workflows/{workflow-id}/execute

docs.title: Step 3: Understand the Workflow
docs.text: Let's break down what each node does:

docs.title: Start Node
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.start",
  "parameters": {}
}

The Start node is the entry point. It passes through any input data or creates an empty item if none is provided.

docs.title: HTTP Request Node
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
  "parameters": {
    "url": "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1",
    "method": "GET"
  }
}

Makes an HTTP GET request and returns the response. The response JSON is available as $json in downstream nodes.

docs.title: Set Node
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.set",
  "parameters": {
    "assignments": [
      {"name": "title", "value": "={{ $json.title }}"},
      {"name": "summary", "value": "Post ID: {{ $json.id }} by User {{ $json.userId }}"}
    ]
  }
}

Transforms data by setting new fields. Uses expressions (={{ }} or {{ }}) to reference data from previous nodes.

docs.title: Connections
docs.text:
{
  "Start": {
    "main": [[{"node": "Fetch Posts", "type": "main", "index": 0}]]
  },
  "Fetch Posts": {
    "main": [[{"node": "Format Output", "type": "main", "index": 0}]]
  }
}

Defines the data flow between nodes. Each connection specifies:

  • Source node name
  • Connection type (main for data flow)
  • Target node and input index

docs.title: Adding a Webhook Trigger
docs.text:
Replace the Start node with a Webhook to trigger the workflow via HTTP:

{
  "id": "webhook",
  "name": "Webhook",
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.webhook",
  "position": [250, 300],
  "parameters": {
    "path": "/my-workflow",
    "httpMethod": "POST"
  }
}

Now you can trigger the workflow with:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/webhook/my-workflow \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"custom": "data"}'

docs.title: Adding Error Handling
docs.text:
Wrap operations in a filter to handle errors:

{
  "id": "check",
  "name": "Check Success",
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.filter",
  "position": [550, 300],
  "parameters": {
    "conditions": [
      {
        "leftValue": "={{ $json.statusCode }}",
        "operator": "equals",
        "rightValue": 200
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you've created your first workflow:

  1. Core Concepts - Understand workflows, nodes, and data
  2. Nodes Reference - Explore all available nodes
  3. Expressions - Learn the expression syntax
  4. Workflow Examples - See more complex workflows

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide covers all methods for installing m9m.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Go 1.21+ (for building from source)
  • Docker (optional, for container deployment)
  • 64-bit Linux, macOS, or Windows

docs.title: Go Install (Recommended)
docs.text:
The simplest way to install m9m:

go install github.com/neul-labs/m9m/cmd/m9m@latest

This installs the m9m binary to your $GOPATH/bin directory.

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
Run m9m in a container:

# Basic usage
docker run -p 8080:8080 ghcr.io/neul-labs/m9m:latest

# With persistent data
docker run -p 8080:8080 \
  -v m9m-data:/app/data \
  ghcr.io/neul-labs/m9m:latest

# With custom configuration
docker run -p 8080:8080 \
  -v ./config.yaml:/app/config/config.yaml \
  -v m9m-data:/app/data \
  ghcr.io/neul-labs/m9m:latest

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
Create a docker-compose.yml:

version: '3.8'
services:
  m9m:
    image: ghcr.io/neul-labs/m9m:latest
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - m9m-data:/app/data
    environment:
      - M9M_LOG_LEVEL=info
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  m9m-data:

Run with:

docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
Clone and build the project:

# Clone repository
git clone https://github.com/neul-labs/m9m.git
cd m9m

# Install dependencies
make deps

# Build
make build

# The binary is at ./m9m
./m9m --version

docs.title: Binary Downloads
docs.text:
Download pre-built binaries from the GitHub Releases page.

Linux (amd64)Linux (arm64)macOS (amd64)macOS (arm64) 
curl -LO https://github.com/neul-labs/m9m/releases/latest/download/m9m-linux-amd64
chmod +x m9m-linux-amd64
sudo mv m9m-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/m9m

curl -LO https://github.com/neul-labs/m9m/releases/latest/download/m9m-linux-arm64
chmod +x m9m-linux-arm64
sudo mv m9m-linux-arm64 /usr/local/bin/m9m

curl -LO https://github.com/neul-labs/m9m/releases/latest/download/m9m-darwin-amd64
chmod +x m9m-darwin-amd64
sudo mv m9m-darwin-amd64 /usr/local/bin/m9m

curl -LO https://github.com/neul-labs/m9m/releases/latest/download/m9m-darwin-arm64
chmod +x m9m-darwin-arm64
sudo mv m9m-darwin-arm64 /usr/local/bin/m9m

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Check that m9m is installed correctly:

# Check version
m9m --version

# Start the server
m9m serve

# In another terminal, check health
curl http://localhost:8080/health

docs.title: Data Directories
docs.text:
m9m stores data in the following locations by default:

Platform Location Linux ~/.m9m/ macOS ~/.m9m/ Windows %USERPROFILE%\.m9m\

Directory structure:

~/.m9m/
├── data/
│   ├── m9m.db        # Workflow storage (SQLite)
│   └── queue.db      # Job queue (SQLite)
├── config/
│   └── config.yaml   # Configuration file
└── logs/
    └── m9m.log       # Log files

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Create your first workflow
  • Configure m9m
  • Deploy to production

docs.title: Nodes Overview
docs.text: Nodes are the building blocks of m9m workflows. Each node performs a specific operation on data.

docs.title: Available Node Categories
docs.text: Category Nodes Description Core 1 Workflow control nodes Transform 9 Data transformation HTTP 1 Web requests Triggers 2 Workflow triggers Database 3 Database operations Messaging 2 Chat platforms AI & LLM 2 AI services Cloud Storage 4 Cloud providers Version Control 2 Git platforms Email 1 Email operations File Operations 2 File system Total 29+

docs.title: Node Structure
docs.text:
Every node follows this JSON structure:

{
  "id": "unique-identifier",
  "name": "Display Name",
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.nodeType",
  "position": [x, y],
  "parameters": {
    "param1": "value1",
    "param2": "={{ $json.dynamicValue }}"
  },
  "disabled": false
}

docs.title: Required Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description id string Unique identifier within workflow name string Display name (used in connections) type string Node type identifier position [x, y] Position in visual editor parameters object Node-specific configuration

docs.title: Optional Fields
docs.text: Field Type Default Description disabled boolean false Skip this node during execution

docs.title: Node Types
docs.text:
All m9m nodes use the n8n-nodes-base.* prefix for n8n compatibility:

n8n-nodes-base.start
n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest
n8n-nodes-base.set
n8n-nodes-base.filter
...

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
Nodes receive input data as an array of items and produce output data:

Input: [{json: {...}}, {json: {...}}]
           │
           ▼
    ┌─────────────┐
    │    Node     │
    │  (process)  │
    └─────────────┘
           │
           ▼
Output: [{json: {...}}]

docs.title: Input Data
docs.text:
• Nodes receive all items from connected upstream nodes
  • Each item has a json property with the data
  • Items may also have binary data for files

docs.title: Output Data
docs.text:
• Nodes can output zero, one, or many items
  • Output items are passed to all connected downstream nodes

docs.title: Expression Support
docs.text:
Most node parameters support expressions:

{
  "parameters": {
    "url": "https://api.example.com/users/{{ $json.userId }}",
    "body": "={{ JSON.stringify($json) }}"
  }
}

See Expressions for full documentation.

docs.title: Credential Injection
docs.text:
Nodes that require authentication can reference credentials:

{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.slack",
  "parameters": {
    "token": "={{ $credentials.slackApi.token }}"
  }
}

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
By default, node errors stop workflow execution. Use the Filter node to handle errors gracefully:

{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.filter",
  "parameters": {
    "conditions": [
      {
        "leftValue": "={{ $json.error }}",
        "operator": "notExists"
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Most Used Nodes
docs.text: Node Type Use Case Start n8n-nodes-base.start Workflow entry point HTTP Request n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest API calls Set n8n-nodes-base.set Set/modify fields Filter n8n-nodes-base.filter Conditional filtering Code n8n-nodes-base.code Custom logic Webhook n8n-nodes-base.webhook HTTP triggers

docs.title: By Use Case
docs.text: Use Case Recommended Nodes API Integration HTTP Request Data Transformation Set, Filter, Code Scheduled Tasks Cron External Triggers Webhook Notifications Slack, Discord, Email AI Processing OpenAI, Anthropic File Storage AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCP Storage

docs.title: AI & LLM Nodes
docs.text: AI nodes integrate with large language models for text generation and analysis.

docs.title: OpenAI Node
docs.text: Interact with OpenAI's GPT models.

docs.title: Type
docs.text: n8n-nodes-base.openAi

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Default Description apiKey string Yes - OpenAI API key model string No gpt-3.5-turbo Model name prompt string Yes - Input prompt maxTokens number No 1000 Max response tokens temperature number No 0.7 Randomness (0-2)

docs.title: Models
docs.text: Model Description gpt-4 Most capable, slower gpt-4-turbo Fast GPT-4 gpt-3.5-turbo Fast, cost-effective

docs.title: Basic Completion
docs.text:
{
  "id": "openai-1",
  "name": "Generate Text",
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.openAi",
  "position": [450, 300],
  "parameters": {
    "apiKey": "={{ $credentials.openai.apiKey }}",
    "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo",
    "prompt": "Summarize this article: {{ $json.content }}",
    "maxTokens": 500
  }
}

docs.title: Dynamic Prompt
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.openAi",
  "parameters": {
    "apiKey": "={{ $credentials.openai.apiKey }}",
    "model": "gpt-4",
    "prompt": "Analyze the following customer feedback and extract:\n1. Sentiment (positive/negative/neutral)\n2. Key topics\n3. Action items\n\nFeedback: {{ $json.feedback }}",
    "maxTokens": 1000,
    "temperature": 0.3
  }
}

docs.title: Code Generation
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.openAi",
  "parameters": {
    "apiKey": "={{ $credentials.openai.apiKey }}",
    "model": "gpt-4",

docs.title: Core Nodes
docs.text: Core nodes provide fundamental workflow control functionality.

docs.title: Start Node
docs.text: The Start node is the entry point for workflows that don't use trigger nodes.

docs.title: Type
docs.text: n8n-nodes-base.start

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Start node initiates workflow execution. It:

  • Passes through any input data provided
  • Creates an empty item if no input is given
  • Must be the first node in non-triggered workflows

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: The Start node has no configurable parameters.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
{
  "id": "start-1",
  "name": "Start",
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.start",
  "position": [250, 300],
  "parameters": {}
}

docs.title: Input
docs.text:
• None required - Creates empty item [{json: {}}]
  • Optional - Passes through provided input data

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
[
  {
    "json": {}
  }
]

Or with input data:

[
  {
    "json": {
      "inputField": "inputValue"
    }
  }
]

docs.title: Basic Workflow Entry
docs.text:
{
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "start",
      "name": "Start",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.start",
      "position": [250, 300],
      "parameters": {}
    },
    {
      "id": "next",
      "name": "Next Step",
      "type": "n8n-nodes-base.set",
      "position": [450, 300],
      "parameters": {
        "assignments": [
          {"name": "message", "value": "Workflow started!"}
        ]
      }
    }
  ],
  "connections": {
    "Start": {
      "main": [[{"node": "Next Step", "type": "main", "index": 0}]]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: With Input Data (CLI)
docs.text:
m9m run workflow.json --input '{"name": "John"}'

The Start node passes through {"name": "John"} to connected nodes.

docs.title: With Input Data (API)
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/workflows/{id}/execute \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"inputData": [{"json": {"name": "John"}}]}'

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text: Scenario Use Start Node? Manual execution Yes Webhook trigger No - use Webhook node Scheduled execution No - use Cron node Subworkflow Yes

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. One Start node per workflow - Only one entry point needed
  2. Position first - Place at the left of your workflow
  3. Clear naming - Keep the default "Start" name for clarity

docs.title: Related Nodes
docs.text:
• Webhook - HTTP trigger
  • Cron - Scheduled trigger

docs.title: PostgreSQL Node
docs.text: Execute queries against PostgreSQL databases.

docs.title: Type
docs.text: n8n-nodes-base.postgres

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Type Required Description connectionUrl string No* PostgreSQL connection URL host string No* Database host port number No Port (default: 5432) database string No* Database name user string No* Username password string No* Password operation string Yes executeQuery, insert, update, delete query string Depends SQL query (for executeQuery)

*Either connectionUrl OR individual connection parameters required.

docs.title: Using Connection URL
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.postgres",
  "parameters": {
    "connectionUrl": "postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb",
    "operation": "executeQuery",
    "query": "SELECT * FROM users"
  }
}

docs.title: Using Individual Parameters
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.postgres",
  "parameters": {
    "host": "localhost",
    "port": 5432,
    "database": "mydb",
    "user": "dbuser",
    "password": "={{ $credentials.postgres.password }}",
    "operation": "executeQuery",
    "query": "SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active'"
  }
}

docs.title: Execute Query
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.postgres",
  "parameters": {
    "connectionUrl": "postgres://...",
    "operation": "executeQuery",
    "query": "SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01'"
  }
}

Output: 

[
  {"json": {"id": 1, "name": "John", "email": "john@example.com"}},
  {"json": {"id": 2, "name": "Jane", "email": "jane@example.com"}}
]

    "conditions": [
      {
        "leftValue": "={{ $json.shouldNotify }}",

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use App Passwords - Don't use main account passwords
  2. Store credentials securely - Use credential manager
  3. Handle bounces - Consider email validation
  4. Rate limiting - Respect provider limits
  5. Test thoroughly - Use test email addresses

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Connection timeout Check firewall, try different port Authentication failed Verify credentials, check app password TLS error Ensure correct port (587 for STARTTLS) Email not delivered Check spam folder, verify sender

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
The node enforces security restrictions:

  • Directory restrictions - Only reads from allowed directories
  • Size limits - Default 100MB maximum
  • Path traversal protection - Prevents ../ attacks

docs.title: Retry on Failure
docs.text:
Use the Code node to implement retry logic:

const maxRetries = 3;
let attempt = 0;

while (attempt < maxRetries) {
  try {
    // HTTP request logic
    break;
  } catch (error) {
    attempt++;
    if (attempt >= maxRetries) throw error;
  }
}

docs.title: Batch Requests
docs.text:
Process multiple URLs:

{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest",
  "parameters": {
    "url": "={{ $json.endpoint }}",
    "method": "GET"
  }
}

With input: 

[
  {"json": {"endpoint": "https://api.example.com/item/1"}},
  {"json": {"endpoint": "https://api.example.com/item/2"}}
]

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use expressions for dynamic URLs - Build URLs from data
  2. Set appropriate headers - Include Content-Type for POST/PUT
  3. Handle errors - Check status codes with Filter node
  4. Rate limiting - Use Split In Batches for bulk requests
  5. Timeout handling - Set appropriate timeouts for slow APIs

docs.title: Operators
docs.text: Operator Description equals Equal to notEquals Not equal to contains String contains notContains String doesn't contain startsWith String starts with endsWith String ends with regex Matches regex pattern exists Field exists notExists Field doesn't exist greaterThan Greater than lessThan Less than greaterThanOrEqual Greater than or equal lessThanOrEqual Less than or equal between Between two values empty Is empty notEmpty Is not empty

docs.title: Type
docs.text: n8n-nodes-base.code

docs.title: Parameters

docs.title: JavaScript Example
docs.text:
{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.code",
  "parameters": {
    "language": "javascript",
    "mode": "runOnceForAllItems",
    "code": "return items.map(item => ({ json: { ...item.json, processed: true } }));"
  }
}

docs.title: Python Example
docs.text:
Python requires python3 to be installed on the host system. m9m creates an isolated virtual environment and executes Python code via subprocess.

{
  "type": "n8n-nodes-base.code",
  "parameters": {
    "language": "python",
    "mode": "runOnceForEachItem",
    "code": "output = {'json': {'original': $json, 'doubled': $json.get('value', 0) * 2}}"
  }
}

Python Requirements: - Python 3.x installed on host - Pre-installed packages: numpy, pandas, requests - Additional allowed packages: beautifulsoup4, matplotlib, scipy, scikit-learn, pillow, openpyxl, pyyaml
